note_data.title: Motion in a circle
keyPoints: **Angular displacement** is measured in radians where 2π radians = 360°
**Angular velocity** ω is the rate of change of angular displacement with time, measured in rad s⁻¹
For uniform circular motion, the **linear speed** v = rω where r is the radius
**Centripetal acceleration** is always directed towards the center of the circle with magnitude a = v²/r = rω²
**Centripetal force** F = ma = mv²/r = mrω² provides the force needed for circular motion
The centripetal force is always perpendicular to the velocity and does not change the speed
**Period** T is the time for one complete revolution where T = 2π/ω = 2πr/v
**Frequency** f is the number of revolutions per second where f = 1/T = ω/2π

definitions.definition: The angle subtended at the center of a circle by an arc length equal to the radius
The rate of change of angular displacement with respect to time
The acceleration of an object moving in a circular path, directed towards the center of the circle
The net force acting on an object moving in circular motion, directed towards the center of the circle

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Show working clearly with correct formula substitution and units
Start from first principles and show each algebraic step clearly

modelAnswer.question: A satellite orbits Earth at a height of 400 km above the surface. The radius of Earth is 6400 km. If the satellite completes one orbit in 90 minutes, calculate: (a) the angular velocity of the satellite (b) the centripetal acceleration of the satellite
markBreakdown: Mark 2: Correct calculation of ω with units
Mark 4: Correct centripetal acceleration with units

commonMistakes: Never say 'centrifugal force acts outward' — there is no centrifugal force in an inertial frame
Students often forget to convert degrees to radians when using ω = θ/t
Never say 'centripetal force changes the speed' — it only changes direction
Students confuse linear velocity v with angular velocity ω in calculations
Always use the correct radius — for satellites, add height to Earth's radius
examinerTips: This topic appears almost every year in Paper 2, especially satellite motion
Always draw a free body diagram showing centripetal force towards center
Remember to convert time to seconds and distances to meters
For satellite questions, gravitational force provides the centripetal force
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Circle showing object with velocity vector tangent to circle, centripetal acceleration and force vectors pointing toward center, radius r labeled from center to object
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to calculate the centripetal force on a satellite orbiting Earth and explain why it doesn't fall to the surface
O/N 2022 P1 Q8 (3 marks) — multiple choice question about the direction of acceleration for an object moving in a horizontal circle at constant speed
M/J 2022 P2 Q5 (5 marks) — students had to derive the relationship between linear speed and angular velocity, then calculate the tension in a string for conical pendulum motion
O/N 2021 P2 Q6 (4 marks) — question on banking of roads, requiring calculation of the minimum speed to prevent skidding on a curved track

workedExample.question: A car of mass 1200 kg travels around a horizontal circular track of radius 50 m at a constant speed of 20 m/s. Calculate (a) the centripetal acceleration, (b) the centripetal force required. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify given values: m = 1200 kg, r = 50 m, v = 20 m/s
Step 2: Calculate centripetal acceleration using a = v²/r = (20)²/50 = 400/50 = 8.0 m/s²
Step 3: Calculate centripetal force using F = ma = 1200 × 8.0 = 9600 N
Step 4: State direction - acceleration and force both directed toward the center of the circle
workedExample.answer: (a) Centripetal acceleration = 8.0 m/s² directed toward center (b) Centripetal force = 9600 N directed toward center
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct substitution into a = v²/r, 1 mark for a = 8.0 m/s², 1 mark for F = ma calculation, 1 mark for F = 9600 N with correct direction

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates incorrectly stated that centripetal force acts outward' — always remember centripetal force acts toward the center of the circle, never outward
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often confused speed with velocity in circular motion questions' — always specify that speed is constant but velocity changes due to changing direction
Examiner Report 2021: 'Common error was using wrong formula for centripetal acceleration' — ensure you use a = v²/r or a = ω²r, not mixing linear and angular quantities incorrectly
note_data.keyDiagram: Draw a circle with an object at one point on the circumference. Show velocity vector tangent to the circle (labeled v), centripetal acceleration vector pointing toward center (labeled a), and centripetal force vector also toward center (labeled F). Include radius line from center to object (labeled r). Add arrows showing the object's path and indicate that velocity direction changes continuously while speed remains constant.
note_data.quickRecap: Objects in circular motion have constant speed but changing velocity due to continuous direction change. Centripetal acceleration a = v²/r always points toward the center. Centripetal force F = mv²/r provides the inward force needed to maintain circular motion. Remember: centripetal means 'center-seeking' - both acceleration and force point inward, never outward.
